rescue_reasoning: Keep as climate-relevant under Resource Efficiency. The policy explicitly links EPR to emissions by targeting 'products with the greatest GHG impact' and proposes an 'EPR/product stewardship framework' (verbatim_text). EPR is a standard resource-efficiency/circularity instrument that reduces lifecycle emissions through improved collection, recycling, reuse, and design-for-environment. Although the gate reports 'no Resource Efficiency outcome token found', the climate_screen is 'explicit_self' and the text contains an explicit GHG-impact rationale, so the row should remain in the climate dataset.
policy_statement: Enact and implement new EPR/product stewardship framework legislation in 2023 or enact individual EPR laws targeting high-GHG-impact products.
mechanism_description: The state plans to enact and implement an EPR/product stewardship framework so producers take greater responsibility for high-impact products, increasing recovery and driving lower-emissions product design and end-of-life management that reduces lifecycle greenhouse gas emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Same products/services → ↑ reuse/recycling + ↓ virgin material extraction/processing → ↓ energy use & process emissions (lifecycle) → ↓ GHG emissions
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: Producers are set (financially/operationally) to manage products/packaging at end-of-life and meet recovery/recycling/design set.  
(2) Mediating process: Higher collection and recycling rates plus design-for-recyclability/reuse reduce demand for virgin material production and shift waste away from landfill/incineration; upstream manufacturing and downstream disposal processes therefore use less energy and generate fewer methane/combustion emissions.  
(3) Outcome: Net reduction in lifecycle GHG emissions associated with products/packaging (materials production + transport + end-of-life management).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing lifecycle GHG emissions by increasing product recovery/recycling and inducing lower-emissions product and packaging design, thereby avoiding emissions from virgin material production and waste disposal.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism primarily works by reducing material throughput and improving circularity (reuse/recycling), which cuts upstream energy and process emissions across the product lifecycle.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although outcomes include direct GHG mitigation, the defining mechanism is delivering the same functionality with fewer virgin materials and less waste—i.e., resource efficiency as the dominant pathway.  
3) Edge cases applied: If an EPR program primarily causes substitution to higher-carbon materials, increases transport emissions substantially, or focuses on toxic pollution without improving recovery, climate benefits could be smaller; however the canonical mechanism specified is explicitly “product_lifecycle_emissions_reduction,” aligning with resource-efficiency-driven lifecycle reductions.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy that fits this canonical mechanism (EPR → lifecycle emissions reduction) fundamentally operates through shifting producer incentives to improve recovery and design, reducing virgin production and disposal emissions—consistent with Resource Efficiency as the primary category.  
5) Note on secondary assignment: Any secondary subtypes (e.g., methane avoidance vs. industrial decarbonization vs. recycling infrastructure) depend on the specific covered products/materials and program design and are deferred to policy-level evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some EPR schemes can increase emissions if compliance relies on long-distance transport/export, energy-intensive processing, or material substitution with higher embodied carbon; conversely, EPR aimed at food/organics would shift toward methane-avoidance mitigation. Classification here assumes the typical/intentional pathway of improved circularity and reduced virgin-material demand.
key_indicators: “enact and implement new legislation”; “creates an EPR/product stewardship framework”; “individual legislation should be enacted targeting products with the greatest GHG impact”; “EPR laws”; “products with the greatest GHG impact”
co_benefits: Reduced landfill waste and local pollution; improved litter prevention and marine debris reduction; potential toxics reduction from better management of batteries/refrigerants; resource conservation.
instance_edge_case_notes: EPR frameworks can include targets/fees/reporting, but the text primarily specifies passing legislation (mandate) rather than a specific performance standard or pricing mechanism; classification hinges on the legal requirement to establish EPR.
